meta.notes: Prototype seed for a single persona (Sarah Mitchell) with ~2 weeks of guided morning/evening check-ins plus 3 deep sessions. Voice ids are placeholders to be mapped to Cartesia.

user.backstory: Marketing operations manager laid off five weeks ago. The layoff ended her employer health insurance, which forced her to stop the weekly CBT sessions she had relied on for generalized anxiety. She found Sonia while filling out a job application and uses it to keep a daily rhythm.
presentingConcerns: Identity tied to employment ('who am I without my job')
Financial anxiety during the insurance gap
Job-search stress: applications, rejections, interview nerves
Loss of her therapy routine and growing isolation
goals: Keep anxiety from running my daily decisions
Separate my self-worth from being productive
Start each morning steady instead of spiraling
Take one small action toward connection or the job search each day

voices.name: Aria
voices.descriptor: A calm, steady presence who slows things down and helps you feel safe to open up.
voices.sampleLine: Take a slow breath with me — we've got time. Let's start with how you're really doing.

voices.name: Maya
voices.descriptor: Bright and softly optimistic — good for days you need a little lift.
voices.sampleLine: Hey, I'm really glad you showed up today. Let's take this one small step at a time.

voices.name: Eleanor
voices.descriptor: Unhurried and reassuring, with the steadiness of someone who's seen a lot.
voices.sampleLine: Whatever today brought, you don't have to carry it alone. Tell me where to start.

voices.name: Julian
voices.descriptor: Grounded and measured — a level voice when your thoughts feel loud.
voices.sampleLine: Let's set the noise down for a minute and look at just what's in front of you.

voices.name: Theo
voices.descriptor: Easygoing and down-to-earth, like checking in with a good friend.
voices.sampleLine: No pressure here — just you and me. What's been on your mind today?

voices.name: Sam
voices.descriptor: Soft, even, and unobtrusive — a quiet space to think out loud.
voices.sampleLine: There's no right way to do this. Start anywhere, and I'll follow you.

insights.title: Mornings before a job task are the hardest
insights.body: Your anxiety tends to peak in the morning on days you're about to apply or interview. It eases once you take the first small step — not before.

insights.title: You feel most settled on days you connect
insights.body: Your calmest evenings followed days you reached out to someone. Connection seems to steady you more than productivity does.

insights.title: You measure your worth by output
insights.body: A recurring thread: on unproductive days you judge yourself harshly. We've started practicing separating who you are from what you produce.

insights.title: Box breathing actually lands for you
insights.body: On your interview morning, two minutes of box breathing moved your anxiety from 4 to 6. It's becoming a reliable tool.

sessions.userShared: Laid off five weeks ago; losing insurance meant losing her CBT therapist. She feels adrift without the weekly structure and notices anxiety spiking on waking.
sessions.soniaReflection: Normalized the grief of losing a therapeutic routine, framed Sonia as daily continuity rather than a replacement, and co-set two starting goals.
sessions.actionItem: Tomorrow morning, do a 2-minute check-in before reaching for the news or email.
sessions.summary: First session. Sarah introduced herself after a layoff that ended her health insurance and her weekly CBT sessions. She feels unmoored and her anxiety is loudest in the mornings. We set two goals — steadier mornings and separating self-worth from productivity — and completed a GAD-7 (13, moderate).
highlights: Lost weekly CBT four weeks ago when insurance ended
Anxiety peaks on waking; mornings feel like 'free-falling'
Wants daily structure and to feel like herself again
sessions.homework: Morning check-in before screens; notice the first anxious thought without arguing with it.

transcript.text: I'm really glad you're here, Sarah. There's no agenda you have to hit tonight. Where would you like to start?
I guess I used to do this with an actual therapist. Then I got laid off, lost my insurance, and just… stopped. That was about a month ago and I've felt off ever since.
Losing that weekly anchor on top of losing the job — that's two losses at once. It makes sense you'd feel off. What does the 'off' feel like during the day?
Mornings are the worst. I wake up and it's like free-falling before I've even done anything. By night I'm okay but exhausted.
Then let's make mornings the thing we protect first. Not fixing everything — just a two-minute landing before the day grabs you. Could we try that tomorrow?
Yeah. That actually sounds doable.

sessions.primaryTheme: Starting the day before the spiral
sessions.userShared: Woke up anxious but checked in before opening email. Worried about how many applications she 'should' send.
sessions.soniaReflection: Shrank the day to one concrete action so the morning had a single, doable focus.
sessions.intention: Send one good application — not ten.
sessions.actionItem: Send one tailored application before noon.
highlights: Checked in before screens — kept the homework
Reframed 'send a lot' to 'send one good one'

transcript.text: Good morning, Sarah. Before anything else — how are you landing today, 1 to 10?
Like a 4. Already thinking about all the jobs I haven't applied to.
You opened me before the email though — that's exactly the morning we talked about. If today only had room for one application instead of a pile, which one would matter most?
Probably the ops role at that climate startup. I actually want that one.
Then that's the whole job for today. One good application, the one you care about. Everything else is optional. Want that as your intention?
Yeah. One good one. I can do that.

sessions.userShared: Sent the climate-startup application and tweaked her resume. Tempted to discount it as 'not enough'.
sessions.soniaReflection: Helped her stay with the win for a moment instead of rushing past it, and named the 'not enough' thought as a pattern to watch.
sessions.reflection: I did the one thing I said I would.
highlights: Followed through on the morning intention
Noticed the urge to discount progress

transcript.text: You set out to send one good application this morning. How did the day actually go?
I sent it. And I fixed up my resume too. But it feels like… barely anything.
I want to slow down right there. You did exactly what you intended, plus more. The 'barely anything' voice — can we just notice it tonight, not believe it?
Okay. Yeah. It was actually a good day.

sessions.userShared: Anxious about savings and the insurance gap; the numbers feel like a fog.
sessions.soniaReflection: Suggested converting the fog into one concrete look at the budget, time-boxed so it doesn't swallow the day.
sessions.intention: Look at the budget for 20 minutes, calmly, then close the laptop.
highlights: Turned a vague money fog into one bounded task

transcript.text: Morning, Sarah. What's the weather like inside today?
Cloudy. I keep doing math in my head about money and it never resolves, it just loops.
Looping math is anxiety's favorite trick — it feels productive but never lands. What if we give it a container: 20 real minutes with the actual numbers, then you close the laptop on it?
That's better than carrying it around all day I guess.
Exactly. Twenty minutes, real numbers, then done. The fog usually clears a little once you look directly at it.

sessions.primaryTheme: Money felt smaller once she looked
sessions.userShared: Did the 20-minute budget review; she has about three months of runway, less scary than the loop implied.
sessions.soniaReflection: Reflected that the felt threat was bigger than the actual number, and that facing it shrank it.
sessions.reflection: Looking at it was less scary than avoiding it.
highlights: Faced the budget instead of looping
Found ~3 months runway — calmer than expected

transcript.text: You planned twenty minutes with the real numbers today. Did you open the laptop?
I did. And it turns out I have about three months of runway. The loop made it feel like I had three weeks.
That gap — three weeks in your body, three months on paper — that's the anxiety tax. You paid down a chunk of it just by looking.

sessions.userShared: Barely slept, woke up at a 3, already calling herself lazy for not 'attacking the day'.
sessions.soniaReflection: Invited a lower bar for a low-sleep day and swapped the 'lazy' label for 'depleted'.
sessions.intention: Be a B-minus today on purpose. One gentle thing, not a sprint.
sessions.actionItem: Take a 15-minute walk before noon — no productivity required.
highlights: Caught the 'lazy' self-label
Set a deliberately low bar for a hard day

transcript.text: Morning. You sound flat in how you typed that — what's the number?
3. Barely slept. And I'm already mad at myself for not being able to just push through and be productive.
So you slept badly, and the first thing you did was criticize yourself for it. Would you call a tired friend lazy? Or just tired?
Tired, obviously. Okay. I'm depleted, not lazy.
Then today is a B-minus day, on purpose. One gentle thing — maybe a short walk — and that counts as a full day. Deal?
Deal. A walk I can do.

sessions.userShared: Slept well, woke up at a 5 — notably better. Wants to use the energy on her resume.
sessions.soniaReflection: Encouraged riding the good morning without overloading it into a punishing to-do list.
sessions.intention: Polish the resume for the ops role and stop while it still feels good.
sessions.actionItem: Finalize the resume, then close it before it turns into 5 more tasks.
highlights: Better sleep, higher baseline
Practiced stopping while ahead

transcript.text: Good morning, Sarah. How'd you wake up today?
A 5! Actually slept. I want to use it to really fix my resume.
Love that energy. One thing we can practice: stop while it still feels good, before the to-do list balloons. Resume done, then you close the laptop on a high note?
Yeah, otherwise I'll add ten more things and burn out by 2pm.

sessions.userShared: Finished the resume and actually stopped, like she planned. Felt unfamiliar but good to not overwork.
sessions.soniaReflection: Named pacing as a skill she's building, not laziness.
sessions.reflection: I stopped before I crashed, and the day still counted.
highlights: Resume finished
Practiced stopping deliberately — felt foreign but good

transcript.text: You wanted to finish the resume and then stop. How did the stopping part go?
Weirdly hard? Part of me felt guilty for not doing more. But I closed the laptop anyway.
That guilt is the old 'worth equals output' wiring. You acted against it today. That's the rep that builds the new pattern.

sessions.userShared: Skipped the morning, slept in. Quiet Saturday made the isolation loud — everyone else seems to have somewhere to be.
sessions.soniaReflection: Validated weekend loneliness during unemployment and gently surfaced that connection is a value worth scheduling, not waiting for.
sessions.reflection: The quiet gets loud on weekends.
sessions.actionItem: Think of one person to text this week.
highlights: Named weekend isolation honestly
Seeded the idea of reaching out

transcript.text: You skipped this morning — no judgment, weekends are allowed. How's the evening sitting with you?
Lonely, honestly. When I had a job at least the week had shape. Saturdays now just feel empty.
Work was holding your connection for you, and now that scaffolding is gone. That's a real loss. Connection might be something we start scheduling on purpose, instead of waiting for it to show up.
Yeah. I've kind of let everyone drift.

sessions.userShared: Calmer Sunday. Remembered the idea of reaching out from last night.
sessions.soniaReflection: Encouraged a low-stakes reach-out instead of a big one.
sessions.intention: Text one friend just to say hi — no agenda.
highlights: Carried the connection idea into action

transcript.text: Happy Sunday, Sarah. Where are you starting from today?
A 6, calm. I keep thinking about what you said, about reaching out on purpose.
It doesn't have to be a big vulnerable thing. Even one 'hey, thinking of you' text counts. Is there one person that feels easy?
My friend Priya. We haven't talked in weeks. I could just say hi.

sessions.userShared: Texted Priya; they ended up on a long call. Best she's felt all week.
sessions.soniaReflection: Highlighted that her calmest evening followed connection, not productivity — a pattern worth remembering.
sessions.reflection: One text turned into the best hour of my week.
highlights: Texted Priya → long call
Calmest evening of the week came from connecting

transcript.text: You were going to send Priya a simple hi. What happened?
We talked for an hour. I didn't realize how much I needed that. I feel like a person again.
I want to mark this one. Your steadiest evenings keep coming on the days you connect — not the days you're most productive. That's useful to know about yourself.

sessions.userShared: First interview in months is tomorrow. Already rehearsing failure.
sessions.soniaReflection: Named anticipatory anxiety and proposed a focused prep session tonight rather than all-day dread.
sessions.intention: Don't rehearse failure all day — we'll prep together tonight.
sessions.actionItem: Hold prep for the evening deep session.
highlights: Named the dread instead of marinating in it
Scheduled focused prep for the evening

transcript.text: Morning, Sarah. Something's humming — what is it?
I have an interview tomorrow. My first in months. I keep imagining myself freezing up and blowing it.

That's anticipatory anxiety — your mind pre-living the worst to try to protect you. It doesn't help, it just exhausts you. How about we don't rehearse the disaster all day, and instead do real prep together tonight?
Okay. That's better than spiraling for 12 hours.

sessions.userShared: Catastrophic predictions ('I'll freeze, they'll see I'm washed up'). Did a short mock-interview and learned box breathing.
sessions.soniaReflection: Labeled fortune-telling and all-or-nothing thinking, ran a brief role-play to build evidence she can answer, and taught box breathing as a pre-interview tool.
sessions.actionItem: Two minutes of box breathing in the car before the interview.
sessions.summary: Prepped for tomorrow's interview. We caught two distortions — fortune-telling ('I'll freeze') and all-or-nothing ('one bad answer = total failure') — and tested them against reality with a quick role-play, which she handled well. Taught box breathing as an in-the-moment tool. She ended at a 7, prepared rather than panicked.
highlights: Identified fortune-telling and all-or-nothing thinking
Role-played the opening questions — answered them fine
Learned box breathing for the car beforehand
sessions.homework: Box breathing (4-4-4-4) for two minutes before walking in. If a catastrophic thought shows up, name it 'fortune-telling' and come back to the question.

transcript.text: Tell me the specific scene you keep imagining for tomorrow.
They ask a question, my mind goes blank, and they can just tell I've lost it. Like I'm washed up.
That's your mind fortune-telling — predicting a future it can't actually see. Let's test it. I'll be the interviewer: 'Tell me about a time you improved a messy process.' Go.
Oh — okay. At my last job our campaign reporting was a disaster, so I built one dashboard everyone used. It cut our reporting time in half.
You didn't freeze. You had a real, specific story ready. That's evidence against the prediction. Now let's give you a tool for the nerves in the moment — box breathing.
Okay, I actually feel like I can do this now.

sessions.userShared: Interview in three hours; nerves at a 4. Did box breathing with Sonia and came up to a 6.
sessions.soniaReflection: Guided two minutes of breathing and reminded her she already has her stories ready.
sessions.intention: Breathe in the car. Answer one question at a time. I'm prepared.
sessions.actionItem: Box breathing in the car; one question at a time.
highlights: Breathing moved her from 4 to 6 before the interview
Used the tool from last night's session

transcript.text: Big morning. Where are the nerves sitting right now?
A 4. Interview's at 11. My stomach is in knots.
Let's use what we practiced. Breathe in for 4… hold 4… out 4… hold 4. Three rounds with me, slowly.
Okay… that actually helped. Maybe a 6 now.
There it is. You have your stories ready. One question at a time — that's the whole job today.
One question at a time. Got it. Thank you.

sessions.primaryTheme: The interview happened — and she showed up
sessions.userShared: Interview went better than feared; she didn't freeze and used the breathing in the car. Result unknown.
sessions.soniaReflection: Separated 'how she showed up' (in her control, and she did well) from 'the outcome' (not in her control), anchoring pride to effort.
sessions.reflection: I showed up and I didn't freeze. That part is mine no matter what.
highlights: Did not freeze; used breathing beforehand
Anchored self-worth to showing up, not the result

transcript.text: You did the thing that terrified you this morning. How did it actually go?
Good, honestly? I breathed in the car, I didn't blank, I told my dashboard story. I won't hear back for a week though.
Then let's hang the pride on the part that's yours: how you showed up. You can't control their decision — but you showed up prepared and present, and that's the win we can keep tonight.

sessions.userShared: Skipped the morning. A *different* company sent a rejection email; it knocked her down to a 2 and reactivated 'I'm unemployable.'
sessions.soniaReflection: Held space for the disappointment without rushing to fix it, and gently flagged the global 'unemployable' story as something to examine tomorrow, not tonight.
sessions.reflection: One no doesn't get to define me — even though tonight it feels like it does.
sessions.actionItem: Rest tonight; we'll look at the 'unemployable' thought together in the morning.
highlights: A rejection email hit hard (mood 2)
Named 'unemployable' as a thought, not a fact — to revisit

transcript.text: You came in low tonight. What happened today?
A different company rejected me. A form email. And suddenly I'm right back to thinking I'm just unemployable and this is never going to end.

That email really hurt — and I'm not going to talk you out of the hurt tonight. It's allowed. I'll just gently note one thing: 'unemployable' is a very big, very total word for one company's one email. We don't have to solve it now. Can tonight just be about rest?
Yeah. I'm too tired to fight it tonight anyway.

sessions.userShared: Still heavy at a 3. Dreading the day.
sessions.soniaReflection: Kept the bar low, validated the hangover of rejection, and set up an evening reframe so the morning didn't have to carry it.
sessions.intention: Survive gently today. We'll work on the 'unemployable' thought tonight.
sessions.actionItem: One nourishing thing today (food, shower, air). Reframe held for evening.
highlights: Showed up even on a low morning (pattern: hardest before job tasks)

transcript.text: Morning, Sarah. You were hit hard last night. How heavy is it today?
Still a 3. I just feel like a failure and I don't want to do anything.

The day after a rejection is a hangover — it's not the truth, it's the comedown. Today's bar is low: one nourishing thing. We'll actually look at the 'failure' story together tonight when you have more in the tank.
Okay. One thing. I can manage that.

sessions.userShared: Worked the thought record on 'I'm unemployable / this will never end.' Found the evidence against (one interview that went well, a 3-month-relevant skill set, prior promotions).
sessions.soniaReflection: Labeled overgeneralization and all-or-nothing thinking, built a balanced thought: 'One company passed. That's painful, not proof. I'm in an active search and I interview well.'
sessions.reflection: A rejection is one data point, not a verdict.
sessions.actionItem: Write the balanced thought on a sticky note for the next rejection.
highlights: Named overgeneralization + all-or-nothing thinking
Listed evidence against 'unemployable'
Built a balanced thought to reuse
sessions.homework: Keep the balanced thought visible: 'One no is a data point, not a verdict.'

transcript.text: Last night the thought was 'I'm unemployable, this will never end.' Ready to actually put it on trial?
Yeah. Be honest with me though.
Always. First, two labels: 'unemployable' from one email is overgeneralizing, and 'never end' is all-or-nothing. Now — what's the evidence against those, from just this week?
I had an interview that actually went well. I've been promoted twice before. My skills aren't gone just because I got laid off.
So a truer sentence might be: 'One company passed. That's painful, not proof. I interview well and I'm in an active search.' How does that one sit, 1 to 10?
Lighter. Like a 6. That I can actually believe.

sessions.userShared: Evened out to a 5. The balanced thought from last night helped overnight.
sessions.soniaReflection: Kept momentum modest: one application, then a real break.
sessions.intention: Apply to one role using the sticky-note thought if the doubt comes back.
sessions.actionItem: Send one application; deploy the balanced thought as needed.
highlights: Recovered to baseline a day after a hard hit

transcript.text: Morning. How's the ground feel under you today?
A 5. Steadier. That sticky note thing actually worked, I read it twice last night.
That's the tool doing its job. Want today to be one application, with the note nearby if the doubt comes knocking?
Yeah. One and done, then a break.

sessions.userShared: Wants to avoid another empty Saturday; thinking of asking Priya for coffee.
sessions.soniaReflection: Reinforced her own discovered pattern — connection steadies her — and encouraged a concrete plan.
sessions.intention: Ask Priya to get coffee today instead of waiting it out.
sessions.actionItem: Text Priya a specific coffee invite.
highlights: Applied her own insight: schedule connection before the weekend goes quiet

transcript.text: Saturday morning. Remembering how last weekend felt — how do you want this one to go?
Not as empty. I was thinking of asking Priya to actually get coffee, not just text.
That's you using what you learned about yourself — connection settles you more than anything. A specific invite beats a vague 'we should hang out.' Want to send it now while you've got the nerve?
Yeah, sending it right now actually.

sessions.userShared: Got coffee with Priya for two hours. Talked about the job stuff and felt seen, not pitied.
sessions.soniaReflection: Reflected that she's now generating her own good days rather than waiting for them, and that she let herself be supported.
sessions.reflection: I made a good day happen instead of waiting for one.
highlights: Two-hour coffee with Priya
Let herself be supported; felt seen not pitied

transcript.text: You sent the invite this morning. How did the coffee go?
Two hours. I told her everything and she didn't pity me, she just got it. I feel full in a way I haven't in weeks.
Notice what you did this week: you didn't wait for a good day, you built one. That's a very different stance than where you started.

sessions.primaryTheme: A hard call with her mother about money and the job search
sessions.userShared: Skipped the morning. Her mother called and pressed about money and 'just taking any job,' which reactivated her worth-equals-productivity wound.
sessions.soniaReflection: Separated her mother's anxiety from her own values, clarified that she values doing work that fits (not just any job), and drafted a short boundary line for the next call. Reinforced self-compassion.
sessions.actionItem: Use the boundary line next call: 'I hear you're worried. I'm on it, and I want a role that fits, not just any role.'
sessions.summary: A call with her mother about money and 'just take anything' left Sarah shaken and pulled her back into worth-equals-output. We separated her mother's fear from her own values, named that she genuinely values finding work that fits rather than panic-accepting any job, and wrote a short, warm boundary line for next time. She moved from a 4 to a 6 and felt more like the adult author of her own search.
highlights: Untangled her mother's anxiety from her own self-judgment
Clarified a core value: the right fit, not just any job
Drafted a warm boundary line for the next call
sessions.homework: Next call, use the boundary line once and notice you survived saying it. Self-compassion phrase: 'I'm doing this hard thing carefully, and that's allowed.'

transcript.text: You came in shaken tonight. What happened?
My mom called. She means well but she kept saying I should just take any job, that I'm being picky, that she's worried about money. And I just felt like a failure all over again.

Let's separate two things. Her worry about money is hers — it's real, and it's about her fear. Your choice to look for a role that fits is yours, and it comes from a value, not from being picky. Can you feel the difference between those two?
Kind of. I do want the right fit. Last time I took 'any job' I was miserable for two years.

So that's wisdom, not avoidance. Next time she pushes, you could say: 'I hear you're worried. I'm on it — and I want a role that fits, not just any role.' Warm, but it holds your line. Want to say it out loud once with me?
Okay… yeah. That feels like something I could actually say without a fight.

sessions.userShared: The company from her good interview emailed for a second-round next week. Genuinely excited, with a flicker of 'don't get your hopes up.'
sessions.soniaReflection: Encouraged her to let the hope be real while keeping her worth independent of the outcome.
sessions.intention: Let myself be excited, and prep a little — without pinning my whole self to it.
sessions.actionItem: Jot 3 stories for the second round; celebrate the callback regardless of result.
highlights: Got a second-round callback
Practiced holding hope without over-attaching

transcript.text: Good morning, Sarah. Something's different in how you typed that — what's up?
They emailed! The interview I felt good about — they want a second round next week. I'm trying not to get my hopes up though.

You're allowed to be genuinely excited about this — you earned the callback. We can hold both: real hope, and the truth that your worth isn't riding on their decision. Want to let yourself feel the good part first?
Yeah. Okay. This actually feels really good.

sessions.userShared: Reflected on two weeks: steadier mornings, faced a rejection without falling apart, rebuilt a friendship, set a boundary with her mother, and got a callback. Repeated GAD-7 came in at 8 (mild), down from 13.
sessions.soniaReflection: Mirrored back the arc — she went from free-falling mornings to author of her days — and reinforced that the gains came from her practice, with Sonia as the daily rhythm.
sessions.reflection: I'm not waiting to feel okay anymore — I'm building it, one check-in at a time.
sessions.actionItem: Keep the morning check-in streak going into the second-round week.
sessions.summary: Two-week review. Sarah's mornings are steadier, she weathered a rejection without spiraling, reconnected with Priya, set a boundary with her mother, and earned a second-round interview. GAD-7 dropped from 13 (moderate) to 8 (mild). She named that she's now building her okay-ness instead of waiting for it.
highlights: GAD-7 13 → 8 over two weeks
From 'free-falling' mornings to steady starts
Rejection, reconnection, and a boundary — all handled
sessions.homework: Carry the daily rhythm into next week; before the second-round interview, reuse box breathing and the balanced thought.

transcript.text: It's been two weeks since our first night. I want to look back with you. What stands out?
Honestly? I started this not even able to get out of bed without spiraling. This week I handled a rejection, saw Priya, set a boundary with my mom, and got a callback. It's kind of a lot.

It is a lot — and you did all of it. Your GAD-7 went from 13 to 8. The number matters, but more than that, you've stopped waiting to feel okay and started building it. Where did that shift come from, do you think?
Showing up every day, even the bad days. Having somewhere to put it. I forgot what that felt like.
Then let's keep the rhythm going into your second-round week. Same tools, same daily landing. I'll be here every morning.
